title: PyTorch AD-based optimization: use torch.stack() not in-place tensor assignment for gradient flow
description: When building batched tensors from loop iterations in PyTorch autograd, pre-allocating with torch.zeros then assigning via indexing (patches[i] = ...) breaks the computational graph and prevents gradient backpropagation.
instructions:
## When to use
- Any time you build a batch of tensors in a loop where the source tensors require gradients
- Specifically in ptychographic reconstruction: _crop_object_patch, _shift_probe, or any batched extraction from nn.Parameters

## Key insight
NEVER do this:
python
patches = torch.zeros((B, H, W), dtype=torch.complex64, device=device)
for i in range(B):
    patches[i] = some_differentiable_op(...)  # BREAKS autograd graph


INSTEAD do this:
python
patches_list = []
for i in range(B):
    patches_list.append(some_differentiable_op(...))
patches = torch.stack(patches_list, dim=0)  # Preserves autograd graph


Or equivalently:
python
patches = torch.stack([some_differentiable_op(...) for i in range(B)], dim=0)


## Constraints
- torch.stack creates a new tensor with proper gradient tracking
- In-place operations on pre-allocated tensors detach the computation graph
- This applies to ALL in-place indexed assignments, not just simple slicing

title: Ptychographic reconstruction loss: global normalization not per-pattern normalization
description: For amplitude-domain ptychographic loss, averaging must be over all pixels and batch positions together (global), not per-pattern normalization followed by batch averaging, which causes incorrect gradient scaling.
instructions:
## When to use
- Implementing loss functions for ptychographic reconstruction with amplitude-domain (p=0.5) formulation

## Key insight
Correct loss (Plan Eq. 5):
python
# I_model_p and I_meas_p are (B, Nx, Ny) tensors raised to power p=0.5
diff = I_model_p - I_meas_p
loss = torch.sqrt(torch.mean(diff ** 2)) / (torch.mean(I_meas_p) + epsilon)

Here `torch.mean` flattens over ALL dimensions (batch + spatial).

INCORRECT (per-pattern normalization):
python
# DO NOT DO THIS:
for each pattern:
    rms = sqrt(mean(diff_i^2))  # per-pattern
    norm = mean(I_meas_i^p)     # per-pattern
    loss_i = rms / norm
loss = mean(loss_i)  # averaging normalized losses

Per-pattern normalization gives different gradient weights to different patterns, distorting optimization.

## Constraints
- epsilon ~1e-6 needed in denominator to prevent division by zero
- p=0.5 (amplitude domain) compresses dynamic range and is standard for ptychography

title: Probe normalization for ptychographic reconstruction intensity scale matching
description: The initial probe must be power-normalized so that simulated diffraction intensities match the scale of measured intensities; without this, the optimizer cannot converge.
instructions:
## When to use
- Initializing probe from a circular Fourier-space aperture for AD-based ptychographic reconstruction
- When PSNR is far below baseline after many iterations (indicates scale mismatch)

## Key insight
After computing the real-space probe from IFFT of aperture mask, normalize its power:
python
# After P_real_space = ifft2(aperture_mask)
mean_measured_intensity = torch.mean(I_meas)  # scalar
probe_power = torch.sum(torch.abs(P_real_space) ** 2)
scale = torch.sqrt(mean_measured_intensity / (probe_power + 1e-12))
P_real_space = P_real_space * scale

This ensures |F[P * O]|^2 is on the same scale as I_meas when object_amp ≈ 1.

## Constraints
- Must be done BEFORE splitting into real/imag parts for nn.Parameter
- If object amplitude is initialized to values != 1.0, adjust accordingly
- k_max=0.25 (quarter Nyquist) is a reasonable default for the aperture radius

title: Ptychographic scan position grid for AD-based reconstruction
description: For single-slice ptychography with N_pos diffraction patterns, object size (Nx_O, Ny_O), and probe size (Nx_P, Ny_P), compute scan positions as a regular grid with sufficient overlap.
instructions:
## When to use
- Setting up scan geometry for ptychographic reconstruction when positions are not provided in metadata
- Input shape (N_pos, Nx_P, Ny_P), output shape (Nx_O, Ny_O)

## Key insight
- Grid side = int(sqrt(N_pos)), e.g., 64 positions → 8×8 grid
- Step = (obj_size - probe_size) / (grid_side - 1)
- For 128×128 object, 64×64 probe, 8×8 grid: step ≈ 9.14 pixels
- Overlap ratio = 1 - step/probe_size ≈ 87.5% (typical for ptychography)
- Use torch.linspace(0, obj_size - probe_size, grid_side) for positions
- Positions represent top-left corner of each probe window
- Use float positions to allow sub-pixel shifting via Fourier phase ramp

## Constraints
- N_pos must be a perfect square for regular grid assumption
- If not perfect square, consider rectangular grid or spiral scan
- Overlap should be >60% for convergence; <50% causes artifacts

title: AD-based ptychography hyperparameters: Adam optimizer with onset scheduling
description: For gradient-based ptychographic reconstruction using Adam, use differentiated learning rates and onset iterations for object amplitude, object phase, and probe to avoid early divergence.
instructions:
## When to use
- Setting up Adam optimizer for single-slice ptychographic reconstruction
- When reconstruction produces PSNR below baseline (12-13 dB range)

## Key insight
Recommended hyperparameters:
- lr_obj_phase = 1e-2, onset = 0 (optimize from start)
- lr_obj_amp = 1e-3, onset = 10 (delay amplitude optimization)
- lr_probe = 1e-1, onset = 10 (delay probe optimization)
- batch_size = 16 (out of 64 total positions)
- n_iterations = 200-500
- p_power = 0.5 (amplitude domain loss)
- adam_betas = (0.9, 0.999)
- Object amplitude clamped to [0.0, 2.0] after each step
- Probe Fourier mask applied every 5 iterations to enforce bandlimit

Key scheduling: Start with object phase only, then enable amplitude and probe after ~10 iterations. This prevents the probe from absorbing object features early on.

## Constraints
- These values assume normalized probe power matching measured intensity scale
- If probe is not normalized, lr_probe may need to be reduced by 10-100x
- More iterations (500+) may be needed for noisy data

title: ePIE ptychographic reconstruction requires rPIE updates and probe support constraints for acceptable quality
description: Basic ePIE with max(|P|^2) denominator and no probe constraints yields PSNR~12.8/SSIM~0.25 on ptychography tasks; rPIE with Fourier support constraint is necessary for convergence to good solutions.
instructions:
## When to use
- Ptychographic reconstruction from 4D-STEM diffraction data
- When basic ePIE gives poor PSNR (<15) despite loss decreasing

## Key insight
- Basic ePIE uses denominator max(|P|^2) which gives uniform step size across the probe footprint. This is suboptimal.
- rPIE uses denominator (|P|^2 + delta) where delta = (1-alpha)*max(|P|^2), providing spatially-varying updates that converge faster.
- Critical constraints that MUST be applied every iteration:
  1. Probe Fourier support: FFT probe, multiply by aperture mask, IFFT back. Without this, probe energy leaks outside physical aperture and reconstruction diverges.
  2. Object amplitude clipping: clip |O| to [0.0, 1.5] to prevent runaway amplitudes.
  3. Probe power conservation: renormalize probe power to match mean measured intensity.
- Use alpha=0.9, beta=0.9 for rPIE step sizes.
- Need 500+ iterations, not 200.

## Constraints
- These improvements alone may not be sufficient if scan positions or probe radius are wrong
- Multi-slice objects require different treatment

title: Probe aperture radius estimation from PACBED for ptychography initialization
description: Incorrect probe radius initialization causes ptychographic reconstruction to converge to wrong solution; estimate radius from position-averaged CBED (PACBED) azimuthal profile.
instructions:
## When to use
- Ptychographic reconstruction where probe parameters are unknown
- When probe_radius is a free parameter that significantly affects reconstruction quality

## Key insight
- Compute PACBED: I_pacbed = mean(I_meas, axis=0) over all scan positions
- Compute azimuthal average of I_pacbed as function of radius from center
- Find radius where azimuthal average drops to 5% of maximum → this is the probe aperture radius
- Fallback to radius=16 (for 64x64 detector) if estimate is unreasonable (<5 or >30)
- Better approach: try multiple candidate radii [10, 13, 16, 19, 22, 25], run 30 iterations of rPIE with each, pick the one with lowest loss
- Probe construction: create disk mask in Fourier space at estimated radius, ifftshift, IFFT2 to get real-space probe, then scale so sum(|FFT2(P)|^2) = mean(sum(I_meas[j]))

## Constraints
- This assumes a round aperture (no aberrations in initial estimate)
- For aberrated probes, the PACBED shape may be non-circular

title: Ptychography scan position computation for regular grid
description: Scan positions must be computed as top-left crop coordinates covering the object-probe overlap region; incorrect positions cause complete reconstruction failure.
instructions:
## When to use
- Setting up scan positions for ptychographic reconstruction with known grid size
- Object shape (Nx_O, Ny_O), probe shape (Nx_P, Ny_P), grid size (Gr, Gc)

## Key insight
python
# Scan positions as top-left crop coordinates for 8x8 grid
# object 128x128, probe 64x64
Nx_O, Ny_O = 128, 128
Nx_P, Ny_P = 64, 64
grid_rows, grid_cols = 8, 8

max_row = Nx_O - Nx_P  # = 64
max_col = Ny_O - Ny_P  # = 64

row_pos = np.linspace(0, max_row, grid_rows)  # [0, 9.14, ..., 64]
col_pos = np.linspace(0, max_col, grid_cols)

col_grid, row_grid = np.meshgrid(col_pos, row_pos)
positions = np.stack([row_grid.ravel(), col_grid.ravel()], axis=1)  # (64, 2)
crop_positions = np.round(positions).astype(int)
crop_positions = np.clip(crop_positions, 0, [max_row, max_col])

- Step size = 64/7 ≈ 9.14 pixels gives ~86% overlap — sufficient for ptychography
- Positions are (row, col) = top-left corner of probe crop window in object array

## Constraints
- Positions must be clipped to valid range [0, object_size - probe_size]
- For non-square grids, compute row and col positions independently

title: Ptychography phase output: use mean subtraction not plane-fit ramp removal
description: Aggressive phase ramp removal (full linear plane fit) can destroy low-frequency phase information in ptychographic reconstructions; use only mean phase subtraction.
instructions:
## When to use
- Post-processing the reconstructed object phase before outputting the result
- When SSIM is low despite reasonable-looking reconstruction

## Key insight
- Phase from ptychography has an arbitrary global offset (gauge freedom)
- Only subtract the mean phase: `phase = np.angle(O); phase -= np.mean(phase)`
- Do NOT fit and remove a linear plane (ax + by + c) — this removes real phase gradients that may be part of the ground truth
- If the evaluation metric compares against a reference that also has arbitrary offset, mean subtraction is sufficient

## Constraints
- If there is a known phase ramp artifact from position errors, targeted ramp removal may be appropriate
- For thick samples with large phase gradients, even mean subtraction should be done carefully

title: Complex pipeline with many hyperparameters needs working baseline before adding features
description: When implementing a complex reconstruction algorithm (rPIE + constraints + position refinement + auto-tuning), implement and validate the core algorithm first before adding advanced features to avoid compounding bugs.
instructions:
## When to use
- Task requires implementing a multi-component algorithm (e.g., rPIE + probe support + amplitude clipping + position refinement + probe radius tuning)
- Previous round failed with basic implementation and plan calls for many simultaneous improvements

## Key insight
- Both rounds failed on ptyrad. Round 1 had basic ePIE that ran but gave poor quality. Round 2 planned many improvements simultaneously (rPIE, probe support, amplitude clipping, position refinement, multi-resolution, probe radius auto-tuning) — this is too many changes at once.
- Strategy: Implement in priority order, validate each:
  1. First: Get rPIE update working (change denominator from max(|P|^2) to |P|^2 + delta)
  2. Second: Add probe Fourier support constraint (FFT→mask→IFFT every iteration)
  3. Third: Add object amplitude clipping (clip |O| to [0, 1.5])
  4. Fourth: Increase iterations to 500
  5. Only then: Add position refinement, probe radius tuning
- Each addition should be testable independently

## Constraints
- Don't skip the core algorithm fixes to add fancy features
- If the architect skeleton is too complex, the coder may produce buggy code for all components simultaneously
